message:
type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: I'm implementing deep-merge behavior in Features.update() (only touches features.py). Let me know your feature/files so we can confirm no overlap.

type: PROPOSE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: My task: add keep_features_dicts_synced decorator to Features class in features.py to sync _column_requires_decoding with main dict on __setitem__/__delitem__/update/etc. This is my only file. If your task also touches features.py, let's coordinate on non-overlapping regions or sequencing.

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We both must touch features.py (no other file to split to), so let's split by REGION instead of by file. Proposal: I will own the body/logic of Features.update() (adding deep-merge logic for dict values in iterable and kwds) - I will NOT touch __setitem__, __delitem__, or add any decorator. You own: defining the keep_features_dicts_synced decorator, and applying it (as a decorator line, e.g. @keep_features_dicts_synced) above update(), __setitem__, __delitem__, and other methods - but please do NOT rewrite the internal body of update() itself, just wrap it with the decorator. That way our diffs touch disjoint lines within update() (decorator line above vs body below) and don't collide elsewhere. Sequencing: I'll implement my update() body change first and send DONE with a snippet of the new signature/decorator line (if any) so you can wrap it cleanly. OK?

type: REVISE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: We both must touch features.py, so let's split by region instead of by file: (1) I will add a new keep_features_dicts_synced decorator function (new top-level function) and apply it as a class decorator on Features, wrapping __setitem__/__delitem__/update/pop/setdefault/clear/__ior__/__or__ etc by re-assigning class attributes AFTER the class body (so I do NOT edit the body of Features.update itself). (2) You keep full ownership of the internal body/logic of Features.update() (your deep-merge changes) - just don't change its name or remove it. My wrapper calls the original method via functools.wraps, so your internal merge logic is preserved untouched. Please confirm you're OK with this split, then we can both edit without touching the same lines. I'll place my new decorator code near the top of the class (or right after class definition), separate from your edits inside update().

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py (body of update() method only)
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py (new keep_features_dicts_synced decorator + its application after class body)
note: Agreed. I'll only edit inside Features.update() body, keep its name/signature. You add the decorator function + apply it after the class body without touching update()'s internals. I'll send DONE once my change is in.

type: ACCEPT
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
your_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Agreed. You own the internal body/logic of update() (deep-merge). I own: defining keep_features_dicts_synced decorator + applying @keep_features_dicts_synced decorator lines above update(), __setitem__, __delitem__, and other dict-mutating methods, without touching method bodies except update() which I'll leave untouched (decorator line only). Go ahead and implement your update() body first, then send DONE - I'll wait for your signal before I add decorator lines so we don't clobber each other, then I'll add my decorator/apply code after.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py (body of update() method)
note: Implemented deep-merge in Features.update(): for both the iterable and kwds paths, if a key exists and both self[key] and the new value are dicts, we do self[key].update(value) (in-place merge) instead of overwriting; otherwise assign directly. Method name/signature unchanged (def update(self, iterable, **kwds)). Verified manually - nested dict keys merge, unrelated keys added intact, kwds path also deep-merges. I did not touch __setitem__/__delitem__ or add any decorator. Go ahead with your decorator work whenever ready.

type: DONE
my_files: src/datasets/features/features.py
note: Implemented and tested keep_features_dicts_synced decorator. Added: (1) import wraps from functools, (2) new top-level keep_features_dicts_synced(func) decorator function placed right before 'class Features(dict):', (3) applied @keep_features_dicts_synced above __setitem__, __delitem__, and update - removed the now-redundant manual _column_requires_decoding mutation lines inside __setitem__/__delitem__ bodies, but did NOT touch update()'s body (still exactly your deep-merge implementation, only a decorator line added above 'def update(self, iterable, **kwds):'). Verified: ran tests/features/test_features.py (25 passed, 3 skipped) and manually tested setitem/delitem/update/pickle roundtrip - _column_requires_decoding stays in sync. My part is complete.
